title: Marketing Team Sees Intermittent Drops After 'Helpful' Switch Was Added

ticket.summary: Since yesterday afternoon, the Marketing team on the 2nd floor reports intermittent connectivity drops lasting 30-60 seconds, several times an hour. A contractor mentioned plugging in a small unmanaged switch under a desk to add extra ports for a temporary event setup. IT did not authorize or configure this device.

topology.description: The 2nd floor is served by two access switches, sw-fl2-acc1 and sw-fl2-acc2, both uplinked redundantly to core switch sw-core1 with Spanning Tree keeping one uplink forwarding and one blocking for loop prevention. sw-core1 was configured as the intended root bridge for VLAN 30 (Marketing, 10.30.0.0/24) with priority 4096. Yesterday, a contractor connected a small unmanaged desk switch between two wall jacks on sw-fl2-acc1, inadvertently creating a second physical path and re-triggering a root bridge election.

links.label: wall jack B (same room, looped via desk switch)

Jul 13 14:22:07 sw-fl2-acc1 %SPANTREE-2-ROOTGUARDBLOCK: not seen, root guard disabled on Gi0/8

cli_outputs.command: n/a - unmanaged switch, no CLI access
cli_outputs.output: Vendor: Genericom GS-108. No STP configuration possible; device forwards BPDUs with default bridge priority 32768 combined with a low-order MAC that computed bridge ID 4144, unintentionally winning root election over sw-core1's priority 4096 due to a secondary rogue bridge advertising priority 4144 detected upstream of it. Device has no management interface.

monitoring.notes: Dashboard shows the elected root bridge ID for VLAN 30 changed from sw-core1 (priority 4096) to bridge ID 4144 at 14:22 on Jul 13, exactly when Marketing reported the first drop. Ports flap between forwarding/blocking on sw-fl2-acc1 and sw-fl2-acc2 correlate with the outage windows reported by users.

hints: Check which switch is currently the STP root bridge for VLAN 30 — is it the one that's supposed to be root?
The root bridge ID changed at 14:22 on Jul 13, right when the intermittent drops started. Look at what got physically connected around that time.

The unauthorized device introduced a new path with a bridge priority that beat sw-core1's priority 4096, moving the root role away from the core and causing suboptimal forwarding/blocking transitions on the access switches.

solution.root_cause: An unauthorized unmanaged switch was connected between two wall jacks on the 2nd floor, creating an unintended path into the topology whose default bridge priority combination produced a lower bridge ID (4144) than sw-core1's configured priority of 4096. This caused an unplanned STP root election, moving the root role away from sw-core1 and causing repeated forwarding/blocking transitions on sw-fl2-acc1 and sw-fl2-acc2 as the topology stabilized.
solution.fix: Physically disconnect the unauthorized unmanaged switch, and enable BPDU Guard / Root Guard on access ports on sw-fl2-acc1 and sw-fl2-acc2 to prevent unauthorized devices from participating in or winning future root elections.
